content.full_text:
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Lloyd L. Hecathorn 
P.O. Box 1203 
Eureka, CA 
95501 
Dear Mr. Hecathorn: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/Legal 
•• 
Enforcement 
322·5901 
322-644 1 
August 4, 1980 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
This is in response to your letter dated July 3, 1980, 
seeking advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions 
of the Political Reform Act. 
The facts as I understand them 
are as follows. 
Presently, you are president of the Humboldt Bay Power 
Company, a corporation formed for the purpose of constructing 
and operating an electricity generating facility. 
It is 
anticipated that the Company and the Humboldt Bay Municipal 
Water District will enter a long-term contract for the purpose 
of supplying the Company with water, and that the Company 
will probably be one of the larger commercial customers of 
the District. 
On June 3, 1980, you were elected to the 
District's Board of Directors. 
You have asked our office to 
consider the following questions: 
Whether you are prohibited 
from holding office because you are president of the Company; 
how you may avoid violation of state conflict of interest 
laws; and whether Government Code Section 1090 prohibits the 
District from contracting with the Company? 
The general conflict of interest provision of the Political 
Reform Act provides: 
No public official at any level of state or local 
government shall make, participate in making or in any 
way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason 
to know he has a financial interest. 
Gov. Code §87100. 
The Act therefore requires you to refrain from partici-
pating in those government decisions in which you are 


Mr. Lloyd L. Hecathorn 
August 4, 1980 
Page Two 
financially interested; it does not prohibit you from holding 
office because of potential conflicts of interest. 
Government Code Section 87103 provides: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
vii thin the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
twelve months prior to the time when the decision is 
made; or 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public of-
ficial is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management. 
By regulation, the Commission has provided guidance for 
determining when a business entity will be materially affected 
by a governmental decision: 
(b) 
In determining whether it is reasonably fore-
seeable that the effects of a governmental decision 
will be ••• [material] consideration should be given to 
the following factors: 
(1) 
Whether, in the case of a business entity 
in which the public official holds a direct or 
indirect investment of one thousand dollars ($1,000) 
or more or in the case of a business entity in 
which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, employee, trustee or holds any position 
of management, the effect of the decision will be 
to increase or decrease: 


~r. Lloyd L. Hecathorn 
August 4, 1980 
Page Three 
(A) 
The annualized gross revenues by 
the lesser of: 
1. 
One hundred thousand dollars 
($100,000); or 
2. 
One percent if it is one thousand 
dollars ($l,OOU), or more; or 
(8) 
Annual net income by the lesser of: 
1. 
Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000); 
or 
2. 
One half of one percent if it 
is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or 
more; or 
(C) 
Current assets or liabilities by 
the lesser of: 
1. 
One hundred thousand dollars 
($100,000); or 
2. 
One half of one percent if it 
is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or 
more. 
Current assets are deemed to be 
increased by the amount of any expenses 
incurred as a result of a governmental 
decision. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code §18702(b) 
Consequently, if it is foreseeable that the Humboldt 
Bay Power Company will be affected by any District decision 
in the manner described above, you must refrain from partici-
pating in that decision in your capacity as a District official, 
unless it will affect a significant segment of the public in 
the same manner in which it will affect the company. 
I regret that I cannot offer you any advice concerning 
the application of Government Code Section 1090 to your 


Mr. Lloyd L. Hecathorn 
August 4, 1980 
Page Four 
situation because this office is empowered to interpret and 
apply only the Political Reform Act of 1974, Government Code 
Sections 81000 et. seq. 
If in the future you have a question 
about the application of the Political Reform Act to a particular 
decision you face as a District board member or if I can be 
of other assistance concerning the Political Reform Act, 
please do not hesitate to contact me.

/---.)~ 
Dwight i~' Dickerson 
Legal Counsel 
Legal Division 


July 3, 1980 
Mr. Dwight Dickerson 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Mr. Dickerson: 
On June 3, 1980, I was elected to the Humboldt Bay 
Municipal Water District (HBMWD) Board of Directors, 
representing Division 3 of said District. 
I have 
enclosed a copy of page 8 of the minutes of a recent 
meeting of the Board of Directors, in which legal 
counsel for the District has raised the question of 
a potential conflict of interest on my part arising 
from the fact that I serve as President of Humboldt 
Bay Power Company, a potential future purchaser of 
water from the District. 
I, of course, want to resolve 
this matter as soon as possible so that I may be able 
to assume my position as an elected representative 
of the people. 
Specifically, Mr. Angell, in discussing this matter 
with my legal counsel, has raised the issue of whether 
or not under present conflict of interest law in the 
State of California, any contract entered into between 
the Humboldt Bay Municipal Water District and Humboldt 
Bay Power Company would be void and a nullity, because 
of my association with Humboldt Bay Power Company. 
I have no ownership interest of any nature in Humboldt 
Bay Power Company, nor do I intend to have any such 
ownership interest in the future. 
Humboldt Bay Power Company, a Cali fornia corporation, WClc? 
formed for the purposes of constructing a forty megawatt 
wood waste burning power fdcility that will also utilize 
refuse derived fuel (rdf). 
Construction of the power 
company facility is anticipated to commence sometime 
within the next two years. 
Humboldt Bay Power Company 
will most likely be one of the larger commercial customers 


Mr. Dwight Dickerson 
July 3, 1980 
Page 2 
of the Humboldt Bay Municipal Water District, utilizing 
an estimated two to six percent of the Water District's 
delivery capacity. 
A long term water supply contract 
between Humboldt Bay Power Company and the Water District 
is contemplated. 
The Water District has a supply capacity of seventy-five 
million gallons per day. 
The largest customers of the 
Water District are two privately owned pulp manufacturing 
companies, each of which presently has a claim on thirty 
million gallons per day from the Water District. 
Prior to my filing for office, I realized that if elected, 
my position as President of Humboldt Bay Power Company would 
disqualify me from voting as an elected official of the 
Water District on any contracts entered into by and between 
the Water District and Humboldt Bay Power Company. 
Inas-
much as the Water District officials have indicated that 
sufficient water is available, I foresaw no problem should 
I be elected. 
The public was informed of my employment with Humboldt 
Bay Power during the election process. 
My Candidate's 
state of Qualifications which appeared in the sample 
ballot pamphlet made specific reference to the fact. 
A 
copy of said statement is enclosed for your review. 
I would greatly appreciate receiving advice from your 
office as to the following: 
First, my right to assume and 
hold public office as a member of the Board of Directors 
of the Humboldt Bay Municipal Water District; second, how 
I should avoid any violation of the conflict of interest 
statutes of the State of California including Section 1090 
of the Government Code; third, whether or not a contract 
entered into between Humboldt Bay Power Company and the 
Water District would be void simply because I serve as an 
elected member of the Board of Directors of the Water 
District, and also serve as President of Humboldt Bay 
Power Company; fourth, whether there are any other issues, 
other than the water contract with Humboldt Bay Power 
Company, on which I should abstain and disqualify myself 
from voting. 
For your additional information, the Water District has 
submitted their conflict of interest code to the Humboldt 


Mr. Dwight Dickerson 
July 3, 1980 
Page 3 
county Board of Supervisors for approval. 
As of this 
date, the Humboldt County Board of Supervisors has 
not acted upon this matter. 
I enclose for your 
review a copy of the proposed Water District conflict 
of interest code. 
Your advice on this matter would be greatly appreciated. 
If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to 
contact me. 
My phone number at work is 707-445-5437. 
Your earliest possible response to my inquiry will be 
most helpful.

LEGAL COUNSEL -
RONALD ANGELL OF MITCHELL, DEDEKAM 
AND ANGELL: 
LEGAL COUNSEL ANGELL EXPRESSED HIS CONCERN ABOUT THE 
RECENT ELECTION OF MR. LLOYD HECATHORN TO THE BOARD AS REGARDS 
A POSSIBLE CONFLICT OF INTEREST, BECAUSE OF MR. HECATHORN'S 
INVOLVEMENT IN THE HUMBOLDT BAY POWER COMPANY. 
MR. ANGELL WILL 
MEET WITH MR. HECATHORN AND HIS ATTORNEY TO DISCUSS THIS SITUATION. 
FOLLOWING THE MEETING, MR. ANGELL MAY OPT TO REQUEST BOARD AUTHORI-
ZATION TO REFER THE MATTER TO THE "FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION".

JOHN WINZLER OF WINZLER AND KELLY:

sections.question: Whether you are prohibited from holding office because you are president of the Company; how you may avoid violation of state conflict of interest laws; and whether Government Code Section 1090 prohibits the District from contracting with the Company?
sections.conclusion: The Act therefore requires you to refrain from participating in those government decisions in which you are financially interested; it does not prohibit you from holding office because of potential conflicts of interest. Consequently, if it is foreseeable that the Humboldt Bay Power Company will be affected by any District decision in the manner described above, you must refrain from participating in that decision in your capacity as a District official, unless it will affect a significant segment of the public in the same manner in which it will affect the company.
sections.question_synthetic: Can Lloyd Hecathorn hold office on the Humboldt Bay Municipal Water District Board while serving as president of Humboldt Bay Power Company, which anticipates becoming a major customer of the District? How should he avoid violating conflict of interest laws, and would a water supply contract between the District and the Power Company be void under Government Code Section 1090?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Hecathorn is not prohibited from holding office but must refrain from participating in District decisions that would materially affect Humboldt Bay Power Company unless the decision affects a significant segment of the public similarly. The FPPC cannot advise on Government Code Section 1090 as it only interprets the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (July 3, 1980 letter from Hecathorn) and FPPC response (August 4, 1980 letter from Dwight Dickerson). Also includes board minutes excerpt showing initial legal concern. Extraction focuses on the FPPC response letter as the primary advice letter. FPPC declined to address Government Code Section 1090 as outside its jurisdiction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether you are prohibited from holding office because you are president of the Company; how you may avoid violation of state conflict of interest laws; and whether Government Code Section 1090 prohibits the District from contracting with the Company?

CONCLUSION: The Act therefore requires you to refrain from participating in those government decisions in which you are financially interested; it does not prohibit you from holding office because of potential conflicts of interest. Consequently, if it is foreseeable that the Humboldt Bay Power Company will be affected by any District decision in the manner described above, you must refrain from participating in that decision in your capacity as a District official, unless it will affect a significant segment of the public in the same manner in which it will affect the company.
embedding.first_500_words: Technical Assistance (916) 322·5662 Lloyd L. Hecathorn P.O. Box 1203 Eureka, CA 95501 Dear Mr. Hecathorn: Administration 322·5660 Executive/Legal •• Enforcement 322·5901 322-644 1 August 4, 1980 Statements of Economic Interest 322-6444 This is in response to your letter dated July 3, 1980, seeking advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts as I understand them are as follows. Presently, you are president of the Humboldt Bay Power Company, a corporation formed for the purpose of constructing and operating an electricity generating facility. It is anticipated that the Company and the Humboldt Bay Municipal Water District will enter a long-term contract for the purpose of supplying the Company with water, and that the Company will probably be one of the larger commercial customers of the District. On June 3, 1980, you were elected to the District's Board of Directors. You have asked our office to consider the following questions: Whether you are prohibited from holding office because you are president of the Company; how you may avoid violation of state conflict of interest laws; and whether Government Code Section 1090 prohibits the District from contracting with the Company? The general conflict of interest provision of the Political Reform Act provides: No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Gov. Code §87100. The Act therefore requires you to refrain from partici- pating in those government decisions in which you are Mr. Lloyd L. Hecathorn August 4, 1980 Page Two financially interested; it does not prohibit you from holding office because of potential conflicts of interest. Government Code Section 87103 provides: An official has a financial interest in a decision vii thin the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made; or (d) Any business entity in which the public of- ficial is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. By regulation, the Commission has provided guidance for determining when a business entity will be materially affected by a governmental decision: (b) In determining whether it is reasonably fore- seeable that the effects of a governmental decision will be ••• [material] consideration should be given to the following factors: (1) Whether, in the case of a business entity in which the public official holds a direct or indirect investment of one thousand dollars
embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to Lloyd Hecathorn's July 3, 1980 inquiry about potential conflicts of interest arising from his dual positions as president of Humboldt Bay Power Company and newly elected member of the Humboldt Bay Municipal Water District Board. The FPPC advises that holding office is permitted but he must abstain from participating in District decisions that would materially affect his company's financial interests.
